steps.text: Peer review all sampling techniques before publication.
Address the precise procedures of a sampling technique when developing or validating sampling tools and techniques.
Indicate all physical sampling techniques or collection tools that are used.
Indicate whether the procedure is a field measurement process such as screening or a direct reading.
List and understand the mission requirements.
Evaluate various sampling techniques according to the project goals and requirements.
Choose the appropriate sampling technique to validate for a given purpose using the decision tree in Figure 1.
Prioritize parameters, weighting optimization toward those representing significant programmatic concerns.
Optimize each significant parameter (either serially or using multi-parameter optimization).
Test previously optimized parameters to ensure they remain optimal.
Make necessary adjustments to previously optimized parameters.
Detail in the technique description the conditions and parameters considered, how to ensure conditions exist, how to address non-conforming samples, and how to treat data outside the optimal range.
Note potential discontinuities in optimization parameters in the technique description.
Consider factors other than cost/sample to select the applicable optimization state.
Consider other operational concerns.
Define and reconcile parameters, their performance criteria, and the conditions under which they may be tested based on programmatic components.
Consider acceptable trade-off conditions in advance.
Develop optimization parameters, data ranges, test conditions, and trade-offs in consultation with, and accepted by, the user community.
Document decisions regarding optimization parameters, data ranges, test conditions, and trade-offs.
Provide the user community with information that occurs during the optimization process.
List operational limits in sampling technique descriptions.
Conduct sampling and further downstream processing at lower temperatures (4°C) within hours to days.
Document required or suggested data parameters or sample-matrix parameters in the sampling process description.
Consider the potential impact that sample collection and transportation might have on the parameter of interest.
Record sampling site and matrix parameters, including date, location, time, weather, and other physical/chemical characteristics.
Specify sample collection areas within the collection parameters to determine the final quantity.
Record the volume or mass to establish the basis of the found quantity.
Ensure sampling amounts conform to the sampling plan.
Include the number of people required, training and skill requirements, and initial/continuing assurance of personnel performance in technique descriptions.
Include methods to monitor personnel and corrective actions to take if personnel fail to meet specified requirements.
Establish appropriate safety and health practices.
Develop a safety plan.
Observe all safety procedures within the safety plan.
Repeat the technique or discard/invalidate the results.

Complete field sampling forms (paper or electronic) at the time of sampling, including location, time, date, conditions, field-measured variables, equipment used, sample preparation, and the sampler's name.
Perform field measurements such as pH and temperature on a separate subsample.
Discard the subsample used for field measurements.
Check comprehensively and analyze data by experienced specialists.
Report results accurately, including information that affects interpretation such as sampling conditions or the method of analysis.
Conduct regular quality compliance checks, including annual reviews and QA system audits.
Document reviews and audits formally and make them available to responsible persons.
Correct deviations from required standards.
Include the test method/procedure and details of equipment/locations used, with calibration status.

Describe the technique’s scope and applicability, including range of performance, components to be validated, matrix to be sampled, nature of analytes, range of analyte levels, advantages and limitations, equipment and supplies, and meeting DQOs.
Describe how selectivity was evaluated and how the sampling plan identified and addressed interferences.
Describe the sensitivity of the technique and how the sampling plan addressed it.
Describe how specificity was evaluated and how the sampling plan addressed it.
Describe how the sampling plan addresses viability assays and what the requirements are for maintaining/preserving viability.
Describe sample security from the collection site to the analytical laboratory.
Discuss the number of items or the quantity that constitutes an adequate sample for the technique, and whether the samples are composites or grab.
Describe how the sampling technique matches the performance of the applicable analytical assay technique(s).

preconditions.text: Appropriate analytical assays or set of assays must be present to measure performance measures of sampling techniques.

The specific combination of matrix analyte, sampling technique, analytical assay and other processing or analytical procedures used to develop the measure must be present to interpret performance measures.
Cost or statistical performance of different optimization states is equivalent.
If viability measurements are included or if viable organisms are to be detected
If a split or replicate of a sample is to be sent to a laboratory for analysis
Samples are not collected from a surface area
Prior to adopting a sampling technique
Viability assays are required or applicable.

constraints.text: The analytical method must be constant for optimization to be possible.
Developers should not promise delivery on performance criteria during the parameter-formulation period, as this might influence parameter selection and bias outcomes.
The user community should understand that it is not possible to guarantee that all recognized performance measures and ranges will be met.
Holding time and temperature can affect microbiological samples, potentially causing either an artificial increase or decrease in the number and viability of target organisms.
Field conditions such as meteorological conditions, low light levels, the absence of horizontal surfaces or the requirement to wear protective gear may limit the performance of a technique.
Air incorporated into a water sample during collection may change the pH of the sample from an anoxic environment.
Live organisms such as pathogenic microorganisms create potentially high biohazard and infection risks.
Control and limit exposure to health hazards through protective measures (e.g., gloves and glove boxes, particle masks, protective eyewear).
Observe Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) regulations.
Material Safety Data Sheets on file and readily available
Safe handling of chemicals and organisms
Field measurements must be performed on a separate subsample to avoid contaminating samples for laboratory analysis.
A conductivity measurement should not be prepared with a sample that previously was used to measure pH, as potassium chloride from the pH probe may affect the conductivity reading.
QA system audits should be independent, thorough, and preferably unannounced.
